title: Winterising an off-grid rainwater harvesting system
subtitle: Draining tanks, lagging exposed pipes, and protecting pumps before the first frost
excerpt: A rainwater harvesting system left unprotected through winter will crack pipes, damage pump seals, and split storage tanks if water freezes in them. Winterisation takes 2 to 3 hours and protects equipment that would be expensive to replace. This covers the sequence: when to start, what to drain, what to lag, and how to restore the system in spring.
sourceNotes: CIBSE TM61 rainwater harvesting system maintenance guidance. Rainwater Harvesting Association UK seasonal maintenance advice. RainWater Concepts UK installation and maintenance manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: A removable basket or chamber at the tank inlet that collects sediment, leaf debris, and organic matter before it enters the storage tank. Requires cleaning 1 to 2 times per year.

projectSchedule.title: Drain pipes and open drain valves
projectSchedule.body: Open all drain valves on exposed supply pipes between the tank and any external tap or hose connection. Let pipes drain fully. Any pipe that cannot gravity-drain needs to be blown clear or removed. Standing water in a horizontal pipe at 0°C expands 9% as it freezes, enough to split a standard plastic compression fitting.

projectSchedule.title: Remove and store the pump
projectSchedule.body: If the system uses a surface or submersible pressure pump rather than gravity-fed delivery, remove it from the tank, drain the pump body completely, and store in a frost-free location. Pump seals fail when water trapped inside them freezes. Most submersible pumps can be lifted out in 5 to 10 minutes via the draw cord.

projectSchedule.title: Spring commissioning
projectSchedule.body: In late February or early March when frost risk is low, clean the silt trap, reconnect the pump, open fill valves, and run a first flush cycle to clear winter accumulation. Check all pipe joints for cracks from any freeze events before putting the system back into regular use.

content.text: An off-grid rainwater system typically consists of a storage tank (above-ground IBC, below-ground poly tank, or surface-mounted butts), a first-flush diverter or

, a pump or gravity outlet, and supply pipes. The parts most at risk of freeze damage are: exposed supply pipes, pump bodies, filter housings, and the first-flush diverter. Buried tanks are rarely at risk; frost rarely penetrates 600 mm in the UK. The work focuses on above-ground components.

In the UK, the risk of sustained frost (sustained air temperatures below -2°C for more than 6 hours) begins in November in the north and December in the south. Start winterisation in mid-October to be safe. Do not wait for the first frost to be forecast. By then the work becomes urgent rather than planned. A system on an exposed site (hilltop, north-facing wall, open moorland) winterises 4 to 6 weeks earlier than a sheltered garden in a valley or urban area.
What to lag and what to drain

Lag (insulate): pipes that cannot be drained (e.g. a permanently connected supply line to an outbuilding or polytunnel) with 25 mm pipe lagging foam. Secure joints with waterproof tape. Lag any filter housing that will remain in place.
Drain: all removable pipe sections between the tank and outdoor taps. Disconnect at the tank outlet and tilt the pipe to drain, then store in a shed or garage.

Partially drain or top up: above-ground IBC tanks and butts should be partially drained to leave air space (at least 10 to 15% empty). A completely full plastic tank has no room to accommodate ice expansion at the surface; a tank with headspace copes with surface freezing without pressure damage.
